title: Create Binary Tree From Descriptions
performanceTarget: top 1% execution time and overall performance
note: All three proposals are implemented in C++ because this problem is dominated by direct pointer-table operations and the native LeetCode return type is TreeNode*.

variants.languageReason: C++ direct-index vectors give O(1) value lookup without hashing or GC overhead, while sizing to the observed maximum value keeps the auxiliary tables more memory-conscious than a fixed full-range table.
variants.approachJustification: Scan for the maximum value, allocate compact direct-address node and child-marker arrays, build edges once, then return the parent value never marked as a child.

variants.languageReason: C++ fixed-size array and bitset over the known 100000 value bound avoid the preliminary max scan, hash computation, bucket indirection, and dynamic table sizing on the hot path.
variants.approachJustification: Use full-range direct-address tables, lazily allocate each node once, mark children in a bitset, and scan parent descriptions for the unmarked root.

variants.languageReason: C++ coordinate compression with sort/unique/lower_bound keeps lookup arrays proportional to distinct nodes while still using contiguous buffers and inlined comparisons.
variants.approachJustification: Compress all observed values to dense ids, build the tree through binary-searched ids, and mark child ids to find the root.

reasoningSummary: The root is the only parent value never marked as a child. Bounded values make direct addressing faster than hashing; coordinate compression is the lower-memory alternative when sparse value ranges matter.
timeComplexity: O(n + M) recommended / O(n + V) runtime / O(n log n) memory
spaceComplexity: O(k + M) recommended / O(k + V) runtime / O(k) auxiliary memory
